name: Lockheed Martin Corporation

profile.description: Lockheed Martin is the world's largest defense contractor, operating across Aeronautics (F-35), Missiles and Fire Control (PAC-3, THAAD), Rotary and Mission Systems, and Space segments. Founded in 1912, the company delivered 191 F-35s and 620 PAC-3 interceptors in 2025 and holds a record $194 billion backlog.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Innovation Track Record (2/3): Record F-35 deliveries of 191 jets and 620 PAC-3 MSE interceptors in FY2025, plus the agreement to quadruple THAAD interceptor production and construction of a Munitions Acceleration Center demonstrate sustained platform execution [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): Lockheed Martin lists Autonomy & AI as a key capability area and integrates AI into F-35 mission systems and satellite constellations, though specific AI revenue figures are not publicly disclosed [3]. Market Share (2/2): #1 global defense contractor by revenue ($75B), with the F-35 selected by 19 nations (1,000+ aircraft operational) and dominant positions in missile defense, satellite systems, and combat aircraft [1]. Pipeline (2/2): $194B record backlog (2.5x annual sales) provides exceptional visibility, with specific programs including NGAD next-gen fighter, hypersonics, THAAD expansion to 2,000/year, and F-35 Block 4 upgrades [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Massive R&D investment in hypersonics, directed energy, autonomous systems, and space technologies across all four segments.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): '21st Century Security' vision — protecting national security through transformative defense technologies — is specific and validated by 110+ years of execution, connecting the company's work to a larger purpose of global stability [3]. Community Impact (1/2): Full Spectrum Leadership (FSL) framework embedded since 2006 promotes ethical behavior and community responsibility; sustainability programs and CSR reporting in place [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): 123,000 employees with FSL culture providing a consistent foundation for 20 years; leadership transitions (Taiclet since 2020) have been orderly and planned [3]. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling — Officer Cahill sold $3.0M, Ulmer sold $1.9M, Hill sold $1.6M in early 2026 [6].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically calculated score is strong despite D/E of 338.8% exceeding the 300% floor (scoring 0 on that metric). ROE of 76.9% is exceptional, reflecting efficient capital deployment in a capital-intensive business [6]. Revenue grew 9.1% YoY to $75B, and Forward P/E of 19.6x is attractive on FY2026 EPS guidance of $29.35-$30.25 (up 37% YoY) [1]. The primary weaknesses are profit margin at 6.7% (typical defense contractor) and 5yr revenue CAGR of 2.8% reflecting modest organic growth. Dividend metrics are solid: 62.1% payout ratio, 20+ years consecutive growth, 6.1% 5yr dividend CAGR [6].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Lockheed Martin's core business — designing, building, and sustaining fighter jets, missile systems, submarines, and satellites — involves classified programs, decades-long lifecycles, and highly regulated government procurement that AI structurally cannot displace [1][3]. Building an F-35 or THAAD interceptor requires physical manufacturing capabilities, security clearances, and institutional knowledge that no AI system can replicate. AI actually increases demand for LMT's autonomous systems and advanced mission computing. AI Opportunity (1/1): Lockheed generates AI-related revenue through its Autonomy & AI capability area, including AI-powered mission systems in F-35 Block 4, autonomous ISR platforms, and AI-driven satellite constellation management [3].

infinite_game.summary: Lockheed Martin demonstrates solid infinite game characteristics through its 110+ year history and multi-decade program commitments, but its competitive language focuses heavily on market dominance and defense spending advocacy rather than mission advancement.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): National defense and security is a genuine cause validated by 110+ years of continuous operation, with the F-35 program alone spanning multiple decades and 19 allied nations [1][3]. The '21st Century Security' vision connects technology development to global stability. However, the cause is shared broadly across the defense industry rather than unique to Lockheed Martin.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Full Spectrum Leadership (FSL) framework has been embedded in the culture since 2006, providing a consistent foundation for developing and assessing leaders at all levels across 123,000 employees [3]. CEO transitions (Hewson to Taiclet in 2020) have been orderly. However, defense contractor cultures tend toward hierarchical structures, and no specific evidence of exceptional employee empowerment beyond industry norms.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Lockheed Martin's competitive framing centers on being '#1' and 'dominance' — 'ensure America and her allies can dominate the skies' and emphasis on record market share and backlog [1][3]. This is classic finite game language focused on winning rather than improving. No evidence of management acknowledging competitors' strengths as motivation for self-improvement.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The 1995 Lockheed-Martin Marietta merger created today's company, and subsequent acquisitions (Sikorsky 2015) expanded capabilities. The pivot from legacy platforms to next-gen technologies (hypersonics, directed energy, autonomous systems) represents meaningful adaptation [3]. However, the core business model — government defense contracting — has remained fundamentally unchanged for decades.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CEO Taiclet has committed to the F-35 program through Block 4 upgrades and international expansion despite political scrutiny and cost challenges. The agreement to quadruple THAAD production to 2,000/year and build a Munitions Acceleration Center demonstrates willingness to make large capital commitments [1]. However, these decisions are largely driven by customer (DoD) demand rather than proactive principled bets.

investment_thesis: Lockheed Martin enters 2026 with unprecedented demand visibility — a record $194B backlog (2.5x annual sales, up 17% YoY) and FY2026 guidance of $77.5-80B revenue and $29.35-$30.25 EPS (up ~37% from FY2025's depressed $21.52) [1]. The geopolitical environment is exceptionally favorable: President Trump's proposed $1.5T defense budget for 2027, NATO rearmament commitments, and the agreement to quadruple THAAD interceptor production to 2,000/year create multi-year revenue tailwinds [1]. At 19.6x forward earnings with a 2.15% yield and 22 consecutive years of dividend growth, LMT offers attractive risk-adjusted returns for defense exposure. Record F-35 deliveries (191 in 2025) and 19 allied nations operating the platform ensure a decades-long sustainment revenue stream [1][3].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on structural financial constraints: D/E of 338.8% is the highest among major defense primes, and profit margins of just 6.7% limit earnings leverage even as revenue grows [6]. FY2025 GAAP EPS declined 4% to $21.52 despite 6% revenue growth, reflecting margin compression from F-35 cost overruns and supply chain challenges that may persist. The 5yr revenue CAGR of just 2.8% demonstrates that organic growth in defense contracting is structurally slow — even in an elevated spending environment, LMT guided only 5% revenue growth for FY2026 [1]. The $194B backlog, while impressive, converts over decades rather than quarters, and cost-plus contracting structures limit upside margin expansion. Consistent insider selling ($6.5M+ in early 2026) suggests executives may be taking profits after the 31% January surge [6].
key_catalysts: Record $194B backlog (up 17% YoY) with 1.2x book-to-bill ratio provides exceptional multi-year revenue visibility across all four segments [1]
FY2026 EPS guided at $29.35-$30.25 (up ~37%) as margin headwinds from F-35 TR3 development roll off and segment operating profit grows 25% [1]
Proposed $1.5T defense budget for 2027 and NATO rearmament commitments create unprecedented demand for PAC-3 (quadrupling to 2,000/yr) and THAAD interceptors [1]
key_risks: D/E of 338.8% is the highest among defense primes, creating balance sheet vulnerability in a rising rate environment and limiting financial flexibility for M&A [6]
Profit margins of 6.7% are structurally constrained by cost-plus contracting, with F-35 Block 4/TR3 cost overruns demonstrating the ongoing risk of program execution challenges [1]
5yr revenue CAGR of just 2.8% with FY2026 guided at only 5% growth highlights that even in the best defense spending environment in decades, organic growth remains sluggish
